l1: Question 1 — Is the slowness affecting one user or multiple users?
Question 2 — Is this a physical machine or a virtual machine (VM)?
Question 3 — Is it a client OS or server OS?
Question 4 — Does the mouse cursor still move on the screen?
Question 5 — Does the keyboard toggle caps lock, num lock, or scroll lock lights?
Question 6 — Does the Ctrl + Shift + Win + B shortcut do anything?
Question 7 — Can you ping the machine or access it over the network via SSH, RDP, or UNC shares (\\servername\c$) while it is hung?
Question 8 — Is the screen frozen on the last active window, or did it go completely black or solid white?
Question 9 — Is there a pattern to when the hang occurs?
Question 10 — Does the machine automatically reboot after hanging, or does it stay frozen until a hard power button reset is forced?
Question 11 — Are there any specific peripherals or external hardware attached to the machine?
Question 12 — What changed in the environment right before the hangs started?
l2: Is it a hard hand or Soft hang?
Upon hard-rebooting the machine, is there a MEMORY.DMP or a minidump file located in C Windows
What does Event Viewer show right before the gap in time caused by the hang?
Does a reboot temporarily resolve the issue?
Is the consumption happening in User Mode or Kernel Mode?
Is this isolated to a single machine or widespread?

l1: Question 1 — Is the slowness affecting one user or multiple users?
Question 2 — If you press the Caps Lock or Num Lock keys on your physical keyboard, do the little LED lights on the keyboard actually turn on and off?
Question 3 — Can you move the mouse cursor across the screen, or is the cursor completely frozen in place?
Question 4 — What happens if you press Ctrl + Shift + Win + B on the keyboard? Does the screen flicker or beep?
Question 5 — If you press Ctrl + Alt + Delete or Ctrl + Shift + Esc, does the security options screen or Task Manager eventually appear, even after a long delay?
Question 6 — Can you ping the IP address of the frozen machine from another computer on the same network?
l2: If you try to connect via Remote Desktop (RDP), SSH, or browse its administrative share (like \\computername\c$), do you get a connection error or a continuous timeout?
For virtual machines: What does the hypervisor management console (VMware vSphere, Hyper-V, Azure, AWS) show for CPU, memory, and disk metrics at the exact moment of the hang?
Is the screen frozen showing the exact last application window you were working in, or did it turn entirely black, blue, or solid white?
Does the machine stay permanently frozen until you physically hold down the power button, or does it eventually restart on its own after a few minutes?
Is there a distinct pattern to when the freeze occurs?
Were there any recent physical hardware changes, external devices attached, or BIOS/firmware updates applied to this machine?
Has there been an aggressive antivirus/EDR policy update or a major Windows Update installed within the last 48 hours?

l1: Toggle Lock Keys.
Restart the Graphics Driver.
Force Access to Security Options.
Attempt Remote Interrogation.
l2: Perform a Clean Hard Reset when machine is completely hang.
Disable Fast Startup - Post-Reboot Fix.
Run System File Integrity Checks.
Check Event Viewer for Pre-Hang Logs.

l1: Collect Remote Performance monitor logs in case of machine is pingable.
Configure dump settings and collect complete memory dump.
Grab FLTMC output.
l2: Collect dump by enabling g-flags.
Collect Performance Monitor logs.
Check Process Explorer.
Collect Process dump of culprit process.

steps.action: Run Remote Performance Monitor for affected machine from remote machine in same Network.
steps.expect: Identify the process in Perfomance Monitor logs.

steps.action: Check Task Manager by pressing ctrl + alt + del to observe the process taking any memory spike or cpu spike during hang.
steps.expect: Confirm the behavior of the process how frequently it is consuming.
Crash the machine when the issue will occur next time.

steps.action: Check the behavior in Safe Mode.
steps.expect: In safe Mode the issue should not be present.

steps.action: Capture Memory dump and engage EE or analyse the dump in Windbg using mex.
steps.expect: Share dump analysis with Customer with Cause of the issue.

steps.action: Monitor the machine for a while after Cutomer Performs action plan provided from dump analysis.
